text: The surgeon begins the procedure on the left side, identifying the left ureter. A lateral to medial approach is used for dissection, as demonstrated in Dr. Wexner's video.

The surgeon approaches the descending colon, moving towards the splenic flexure. The omentum is visible and can be detached from the distal transverse colon at the splenic flexure, which is easier in slim patients. In heavier patients, the surgeon accesses the lesser sac to remove the omentum along with the splenic flexure.

After mobilizing the splenic flexure, the surgeon focuses on the right colon, identifying the right ureter. Using a lateral to medial dissection, the peritoneum is scored around the base of the cecum and terminal ileum to access the retroperitoneal plane. The BOVI cautery device is used to achieve a clean dissection plane, avoiding tissue tearing. The surgeon proceeds along the right lateral peritoneal reflection, gently sweeping away filmy tissues to expose the dissection plane.

The surgeon continues the dissection, moving both cephalad and medially, exposing the right retroperitoneum. The right ureter, duodenum, and inferior vena cava become visible as the dissection approaches the hepatic flexure. The plane of dissection shifts slightly more medially at this point.

The surgeon elevates and divides the hepatocolic attachments near the hepatic flexure, completing the mobilization of the right colon. The patient is positioned right side up in Trendelenburg to facilitate this process, ensuring maximum mobility for the subsequent creation of the ilioanal pouch.

The hepatic flexure is positioned below the level of the umbilicus, indicating adequate mobilization of the right colon for extraction. The surgeon then focuses on pelvic dissection, opening the plane and visualizing the left ureter in the left lower quadrant. The left pararectal tissues are scored to access the presacral space, allowing elevation of the rectum. Simple cautery is used to maintain a bloodless plane during posterior dissection, followed by tension on the rectum and dissection along the left lateral pararectal attachments, before moving to the right side of the rectum.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter in the right lower quadrant of the surgical field. The dissection proceeds by scoring the right pararectal tissues to enter the presacral space, which has been previously dissected from the left side. Tension is applied to the right pararectal tissues to continue the dissection. In female patients, the uterus may obstruct the view, and it can be elevated using a Keith needle with transcutaneous stitches through the fundus or broad ligament.

The surgeon carefully delineates the posterior wall of the vagina, ensuring not to damage it, especially in patients with a rectocele. After completing the posterior and bilateral dissection, tension is placed anteriorly to facilitate the dissection. A sizer or rigid proctoscope is used in the rectum to apply traction and delineate the planes. A stapler is utilized through the right lower quadrant port.

The surgeon applies significant traction on the rectum with assistance from the perineum, causing a rim of tissue to disappear into the pelvic floor. The staple line is visible, coning down into the anal canal, leaving one to two centimeters of mucosa in the proximal half of the anal canal.

The surgeon exteriorizes the entire colon and rectum through a peri-umbilical incision, positioning them as midline structures. The small bowel is also exteriorized to check for signs of Crohn's disease, ensuring adequate length for pouch creation beneath the incision.

The surgeon makes final incisions using a peri-umbilical extraction site and two five-millimeter ports at the ileostomy. A drain is brought out through the suprapubic port site, resulting in a cosmetic appearance post-ileostomy closure.

The surgeon demonstrates the extraction of the entire colon and rectum through the ileostomy site after intracorporeal division of the mesentery, avoiding the need for a peri-umbilical extraction incision.

The surgeon exteriorizes a bulky rectum through a standard-sized ileostomy site, despite its inflammation and bulkiness due to disease. A J-pouch is created in standard fashion, ensuring it reaches down to the pubis.

The surgeon brings up the ileostomy and places a clamp across the small bowel. The pneumoperitoneum is reestablished, revealing the staple line positioned well down. The cut edge of the small bowel mesentery is checked for twists by tracing it up to the duodenum, ensuring no loops of small bowel have slipped beneath the mesentery. The anastomosis is created, and the ileostomy is matured.
